title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered on local history projects. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Sheila, The Map Scanner
Sheila scanned old town planning maps for a local history website that the library planned to launch. [47]She learned to place a black sheet behind thin paper so faded street names did not disappear into the scanner light.[/47] The trick made the images clearer, but it also slowed her down, especially with maps already torn at the folds. Sheila had worked in a print shop, and [48]her knowledge of paper sizes helped her choose settings that avoided cutting off the edges of folded maps.[/48] She found it strange seeing streets where houses now stood across former fields, mills and narrow streams. The most frustrating discovery was that several maps had been repaired with shiny tape that reflected badly unless she adjusted the angle. Sheila says digitising maps is not just copying them; it is deciding how future readers will first meet a place they may never have walked through themselves.
texts.B:
### Colin, The Oral-History Interviewer
Colin patiently interviewed older residents about shops that had disappeared from the busy high street before supermarkets arrived. [49]He stopped beginning with dates after noticing that people remembered smells and shopkeepers' voices more readily than years.[/49] Instead, he asked what could first be heard when the door opened or what treats children hoped to buy with pocket money. Colin had been a radio presenter, so he was comfortable with silence and rarely interrupted too soon, even when the pause felt long. [50]His most valuable recording came from a woman who corrected a street name that had been copied wrongly in three books.[/50] He always sent interviewees a copy of their recording, because some had never heard their own private memories treated as evidence by anyone outside their family. Colin now says the work changed his idea of accuracy: emotion and fact were often partners, not enemies in a useful archive.
texts.C:
### Yara, The Photograph Identifier
Yara carefully helped identify people in boxes of unlabelled black-and-white single photographs donated at short notice by a closing weekly newspaper with a dusty basement. [51]She used shop signs and bus routes in the blurred background to narrow down when each picture had been taken.[/51] Her grandmother recognised several familiar faces, but Yara refused to add a name unless two independent sources supported it. That caution occasionally annoyed excited relatives who were certain they knew everyone in the picture. As a police analyst, Yara was used to separating confident claims from reliable evidence under pressure. [52]The single photograph that affected her most showed a flooded playground where her own mother had once repeatedly told her she learned to swim.[/52] Yara says the archive project made family stories feel unexpectedly larger and more public, because private memories suddenly partly belonged to the wider town as well, especially when neighbours recognised places.
texts.D:
### Mikkel, The Exhibition Writer
Mikkel carefully wrote captions for a temporary summer exhibition about the town's old cinema near the market square. [53]He rewrote every display label after a test visitor said the first version sounded as if it was written for experts only.[/53] The new captions used shorter clearer sentences and explained technical cinema words such as projectionist. Mikkel's background in advertising helped him choose lively openings without exaggerating the facts or creating false nostalgia. [54]The hardest object to caption was a torn ticket, because its importance depended on a strike night that needed careful explanation for visitors born later.[/54] He also argued for including ordinary memories of uncomfortable seats and sticky cinema floors, not just glamorous opening premieres. [55]He now asks volunteers to read captions aloud before final printing them.[/55] [56]He believes an exhibition succeeds when visitors start telling their own stories before leaving the final panel and heading back to reception.[/56]

questions.question: used a dark backing to improve the visibility of faint printed details?
questions.explanation: Sheila placed a black sheet behind thin paper so faded street names remained visible.

questions.question: used printing experience to avoid losing parts of large documents during scanning?
questions.explanation: Sheila's print-shop knowledge helped her choose settings that kept map edges visible.

questions.question: changed interview openings because sensory memories worked better than dates?
questions.explanation: Colin stopped starting with dates because people remembered smells and voices more easily.

questions.question: obtained a recording that corrected an error repeated in published sources?
questions.explanation: Colin recorded a woman correcting a street name copied wrongly in three books.

questions.question: used background clues in images to estimate when they were taken?
questions.explanation: Yara used shop signs and bus routes to narrow down photograph dates.

questions.question: was personally moved by an image connected to a parent's childhood story?
questions.explanation: Yara was affected by a flooded playground linked to her mother's story.

questions.question: simplified written material after feedback that it sounded too specialist?
questions.explanation: Mikkel rewrote labels after a visitor said they sounded written for experts.

questions.question: found one small object hard to explain because its context was complex?
questions.explanation: Mikkel found the torn ticket difficult because it needed the strike-night context.

questions.question: checks display text by having it spoken before it is produced?
questions.explanation: Mikkel asks volunteers to read captions aloud before printing.

questions.question: measures success by visitors beginning to share their own memories?
questions.explanation: Mikkel believes success is when visitors start telling their own stories.
